text: The surgeon begins the procedure by mobilizing the diseased left colon, using a lateral to medial approach to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues mobilizing the colon up to the splenic flexure, carefully avoiding excessive retraction on the spleen to prevent capsular tears.

The surgeon begins mobilization of the distal transverse colon, dissecting the greater omentum to access the lesser sac. The dissection progresses towards the splenic flexure, aiming to connect with the previously mobilized left colon.

The surgeon dissects and skeletonizes the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) after fully mobilizing the left colon from the retroperitoneum. An endoscopic stapler is used to ligate the IMV. Subsequently, the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is dissected and ligated with an endoscopic stapler, ensuring the left ureter is not included in the stapler.
The surgeon dissects the middle colic vessels and uses an endoscopic stapler to ligate them, with a twelve millimeter port placed in the left upper quadrant for access.

The surgeon mobilizes the right colon from a supracolic approach, advancing the dissection of the proximal transverse colon towards the hepatic flexure. The duodenum and head of the pancreas are clearly visualized, with careful dissection to avoid injury to these structures.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the right colon using a lateral to medial infracolic approach. The right colon is fully mobilized, and the ileocolic pedicle is dissected. An endoscopic stapler is used to ligate the ileocolic vessels.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the rectum by opening the peritoneal reflection on the right lateral aspect of the upper rectum, followed by the left lateral aspect. The rectum is then circumferentially mobilized in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection of the rectum, taking care to avoid injuring the adjacent vagina. The dissection continues circumferentially around the rectum, with posterior dissection progressing towards the pelvic floor.

The surgeon uses gentle pushing of the tissue to reveal the tissue plane before cutting during the dissection. After completing the intra-abdominal dissection of the rectum, the perineal dissection begins via the intersphincteric plane to enter the pelvic cavity and deliver the specimen. Diseased perianal skin is excised, and the terminal ileum is divided with a linear stapler. The levators are closed with absorbable suture, followed by additional layers of absorbable suture applied to the fat layer and skin.

The surgeon re-establishes pneumoperitoneum to verify the orientation of the mesentery before maturing the stoma. An inspection confirms hemostasis, and a nineteen Blake drain is placed in the pelvis. After creating the ileostomy, a final inspection ensures the correct orientation, highlighting a straight line through the cut edge of the small bowel mesentery down to the SMA origin.
